content.full_text:
State of California
Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. BOX 807 • SACRAMENTO, 95804 • • • 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95314

A-76-05-010

May 24, 1976


Steven V. Seekins, Coordinator
California Legislature
116 Ninth Street
Suite 68
Sacramento, California 95814

Dear Mr. Seekins:

Thank you for your letter of May 5, 1976, seeking advice
with respect to whether the reimbursement of travel expenses
incurred by a state senator in connection with his activities
on behalf of a federal candidate must be reported as income
on a Statement of Economic Interest. The reimbursement for
the expenses to which you refer is made in the context of
political activities performed by the state senator. The
Commission has determined that payments received for political
purposes should be reported, if at all, as contributions under
Chapter 4 of the Political Reform Act and not under Chapter 7
as personal income. See opinions requested by State Controller
Kenneth Cory, 1 FPPC Ops. 137 (No. 75-094-C, Oct. 1, 1975);
Assemblyman Willie L. Brown, Jr., 1 FPPC Ops. 67 (No. 75-055,
Jul. 2, 1975). Furthermore, under the circumstances posited
in your letter, the state senator undoubtedly is providing
adequate consideration in exchange for the reimbursement
received. Accordingly, he will not receive a contribution
within the meaning of the Political Reform Act and, therefore,
will not incur any reporting obligation under Chapter 4 of the
Act. Finally, the foregoing advice would be the same even if
the reimbursement is made by a campaign committee supporting
a candidate for state office.

I hope the foregoing is helpful. If you have any
further questions, please do not hesitate to call me.

Sincerely,

Natalie E. West
Staff Counsel

NEW:av

PETER H. BEHR
CLARE BERRYHILL
LOU CUSANOVICH
GEORGE DEUKMEJIAN
DONALD L. GRUNSKY
MILTON MARKS
JOHN A. NEJEDLY

H. L. RICHARDSON
NEWTON R. RUSSELL
JACK SCHRADE
ROBERT S. STEVENS
JOHN STULL
HOWARD WAY
JAMES E. WHTEMORE

California Legislature

SENATE REPUBLICAN CAUCUS
DENNIS E. CARPENTER
CHAIRMAN

STEVEN V. SEEKINS
COORDINATOR

1118 NINTH STREET, SUITE 66
SACRAMENTO, CALIFORNIA 95814
TELEPHONE: (916) 445-6617

May 5, 1976

Mr. Delbert Spurlock
Fair Political Practices Committee
Conflict of Interest Division
1100 K Street, 4th Floor
Sacramento, CA  95814

Dear Mr. Spurlock:

As a result of our conversation today, I have put the
following question in writing:

     If a State Senator receives reimbursement of
     travel expenses (air fare, meals, car rental,
     taxi fares, tips, etc.) in the amount of $250
     or more from the campaign committee of a candi-
     date for federal office, is the State Senator
     obligated to report these reimbursements as
     income on his conflict of interest statement?

It would be interesting for me to have your response to
the same question with the exception that the reimburse-
ment would come from the campaign committee of a candidate
for state office.

If there are any questions about this, or to the issues
raised by my letter, please contact me.

                                        Sincerely,

                                        Steven V. Seekins

SVS/nf

sections.question_synthetic: Whether reimbursement of travel expenses incurred by a state senator in connection with activities on behalf of a federal candidate must be reported as income on a Statement of Economic Interest.
sections.conclusion_synthetic: The FPPC concluded that such reimbursements made in the context of political activities should be reported as contributions under Chapter 4 of the Political Reform Act (if at all), not as personal income under Chapter 7. Additionally, since the state senator provides adequate consideration in exchange for the reimbursement, no reportable contribution occurs and no reporting obligation exists.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: No formal QUESTION or CONCLUSION section headers present; document is an advice letter responding to May 5, 1976 inquiry from Steven V. Seekins, Coordinator of the California Legislature Senate Republican Caucus. Advice applies regardless of whether reimbursement comes from federal campaign or state candidate campaign. References prior FPPC opinions.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: Whether reimbursement of travel expenses incurred by a state senator in connection with activities on behalf of a federal candidate must be reported as income on a Statement of Economic Interest.

CONCLUSION: The FPPC concluded that such reimbursements made in the context of political activities should be reported as contributions under Chapter 4 of the Political Reform Act (if at all), not as personal income under Chapter 7. Additionally, since the state senator provides adequate consideration in exchange for the reimbursement, no reportable contribution occurs and no reporting obligation exists.

The reimbursement for the expenses to which you refer is made in the context of political activities performed by the state senator. SEEKINS COORDINATOR 1118 NINTH STREET, SUITE 66 SACRAMENTO, CALIFORNIA 95814 TELEPHONE: (916) 445-6617 May 5, 1976 Mr. Delbert Spurlock Fair Political Practices Committee Conflict of Interest Division 1100 K Street, 4th Floor Sacramento, CA 95814 Dear Mr. Spurlock: As a result of our conversation today, I have put the following question in writing: If a State Senator receives reimbursement of travel expenses (air fare, meals, car rental, taxi fares, tips, etc.) in the amount of $250 or more from the campaign committee of a candi- date for federal office, is the State Senator obligated to report these reimbursements as income on his conflict of interest statement? It would be interesting for me to have your response to the same question with the exception that the reimburse- ment would come from the campaign committee of a candidate for state office. If there are any questions about this, or to the issues raised by my letter, please contact me. Sincerely, Steven V. Seekins SVS/nf
embedding.summary: FPPC Counsel West advises that travel expense reimbursements to a state senator for activities supporting a federal candidate are not reportable as income on a Statement of Economic Interest, as such political payments are treated as contributions under the Political Reform Act and do not constitute reportable contributions when adequate consideration is provided.
